content:
Match the properties of nerve fibres in Column I with their explanation in Column II

Column I Column II
i. Irritability a. Halting of nerve impulse transmission due to exhaustion of neurotransmitter.
ii. Synaptic fatigue b. Ability to perceive stimulus and enter into a state of activity.
iii. Conductivity c. Many subliminal stimuli are given in quick succession.
iv. Summation effect d. Ability to transmit the excitation.
Choose the correct option:

options.content: i - a, ii - d, iii - b, iv - c
i - b, ii - a, iii - d, iv - c
i - c, ii - b, iii - a, iv - d

explanation:
Let's match each term carefully:

i. Irritability → Ability to perceive stimulus and enter into a state of activity → (b)

ii. Synaptic fatigue → Halting of nerve impulse transmission due to exhaustion of neurotransmitter → (a)

iii. Conductivity → Ability to transmit the excitation → (d)

iv. Summation effect → Many subliminal stimuli are given in quick succession → (c)

✅ Correct matching:

i – b

ii – a

iii – d

iv – c

Correct Option: C

content:
Match parts of human ear in Column I with their structure / characteristic in Column II.

Column I Column II
i. Organ of Corti a. Dynamic balance is maintained
ii. Semi-circular Canal b. Sensory epithelium over basilar membrane of cochlea
iii. Tympanic membrane c. Connects middle ear to the pharynx
iv. Eustachian tube d. Transmits sound waves to middle ear
Choose the correct option:

options.content: i - b, ii - a, iii - d, iv - c
i - c, ii - d, iii - a, iv - b
i - a, ii - d, iii - c, iv - b

explanation:
Let's match each part correctly:

  • i. Organ of Corti → It is the sensory epithelium over the basilar membrane of the cochlea → b

  • ii. Semi-circular Canal → Responsible for dynamic balance → a

  • iii. Tympanic membrane → Transmits sound waves to the middle ear → d

  • iv. Eustachian tube → Connects middle ear to the pharynx → c

✅ Correct option: Option A (i - b, ii - a, iii - d, iv - c)

content: Select the INCORRECT statement regarding spinal cord from the following.

options.content: Ascending spinal tracts conduct sensory impulses to the brain.
Dorsal root ganglion has unipolar sensory neurons.
Descending spinal tracts carry motor impulses from the brain to spinal cord.
Watering of mouth on sight of food is a spinal reflex.

explanation: Salivation (watering of mouth) is a cranial reflex.

content: Smallest branch of mixed spinal nerve is $\qquad$
explanation: Smallest branch; originates from the 1st thoracic to the 3rd lumbar (L3) spinal nerve. Joins the sympathetic ganglia.

content: Which one of the following is NOT an example of unconditioned reflex?

explanation:
The correct answer is:

✅ Option B: -70 millivolts

Explanation:

The resting membrane potential of a typical neuron (axon) is about -70 mV.

This negative value is maintained by the sodium-potassium pump (Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase) and the selective permeability of the membrane to potassium ions (K⁺), which allows more positively charged K⁺ ions to leave the cell than positive ions entering it. This makes the inside of the neuron negatively charged relative to the outside.

content: Disorder caused due to accumulation of amyloid proteins in CNS is ___________
explanation: Alzheimer's is caused by amyloid protein accumulation in the CNS, leading to plaques and cognitive decline.

content: Which of the following statements is INCORRECT regarding macula lutea?

options.content: It has fovea centralis at its centre.
It has maximum density of cone cells.
It is the area of retina from where optic nerve leaves the eyeball.
It is a place of formation of sharpest vision.

explanation:
Let's analyze each option carefully:

  • Option A: It has fovea centralis at its centre.

    ✅ Correct. The macula lutea is a yellowish area in the retina, and the fovea centralis lies at its center.

  • Option B: It has maximum density of cone cells.

    ✅ Correct. The macula (especially the fovea centralis) indeed has the highest concentration of cone cells, responsible for detailed color vision.

  • Option C: It is the area of retina from where optic nerve leaves the eyeball.

    ❌ Incorrect. The optic nerve leaves the eyeball at the optic disc (blind spot), not at the macula lutea.

  • Option D: It is a place of formation of sharpest vision.

    ✅ Correct. The macula lutea, particularly the fovea centralis, provides the sharpest visual acuity.

✅ Answer: Option C – It is the area of retina from where optic nerve leaves the eyeball.

content:
Select the correct statements regarding sympathetic nervous system.

i. It consists of 31 pairs of sympathetic ganglia.

ii. It is also called cranio- sacral outflow.

iii. It controls body activities like fight and flight

iv. Pre-ganglionic fibres are long and post response. ganglionic fibres are short.

v. Adrenaline is produced towards terminal end of post-ganglionic fibres.
explanation:
i. Sympathetic nervous system consists of 22 pairs of sympathetic ganglia.

ii. Parasympathetic Nervous System is also called cranio- sacral outflow.

iii. Pre-ganglionic fibres are short and post ganglionic fibres are long.

content: In a polarised state of nerve membrane, sodiumpotassium exchange pump actively restores the ions on their appropriate side because ________ against concentration electrochemical gradient.

options.content: $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$is forced out and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$is forced inside the membrane
$\mathrm{K}^{+}$is forced out and $\mathrm{Na}^{+}$is forced inside the membrane

explanation:
The sodium–potassium pump (Na⁺/K⁺‑ATPase) actively transports ions in opposite directions to maintain the resting membrane potential:

  • 3 Na⁺ ions are pumped out of the neuron (from inside to outside)

  • 2 K⁺ ions are pumped in (from outside to inside)

This pumping goes against both concentration and electrochemical gradients, requiring energy from ATP.

✅ Correct Answer: Option A

$ \mathrm{Na}^{+} \text{ is forced out and } \mathrm{K}^{+} \text{ is forced inside the membrane.} $

content: Cranial nerve $\underline{\mathrm{i}}$ is also called pneumogastric, $\underline{\mathrm{i}} \underline{\mathrm{i}}$ is also called vestibulocochlear, and iii is also called Dentist's Nerve.

options.content: i - Spinal accessory, ii - Olfactory, iii - Vagus.
i - Vagus, ii - Auditory, iii - Trigeminal.
i - Facial, ii - Abducens, iii - Glossopharyngeal.
i - Pathetic, ii - Hypoglossal, iii - Vagus.

explanation:
Step-by-step solution:

  1. Let us identify cranial nerves as per NCERT:

  • Vagus nerve (X):

    • Also called "pneumogastric nerve".

  • Auditory/vestibulocochlear nerve (VIII):

    • Also called "vestibulocochlear nerve".

  • Trigeminal nerve (V):

    • Also called "Dentist's Nerve" (because it supplies sensory fibres to the jaws and teeth).
  1. Check the options for correct matching:

  • Option A

    • i - Spinal accessory (XI), ii - Olfactory (I), iii - Vagus (X)

    • These do NOT match the names given in the question.

  • Option B

    • i - Vagus (X) $\longrightarrow$ Pneumogastric ✔️

    • ii - Auditory (VIII) $\longrightarrow$ Vestibulocochlear ✔️

    • iii - Trigeminal (V) $\longrightarrow$ Dentist's Nerve ✔️

  • Option C

    • i - Facial (VII), ii - Abducens (VI), iii - Glossopharyngeal (IX)

    • These do NOT match the names given in the question.

  • Option D

    • i - Pathetic (IV), ii - Hypoglossal (XII), iii - Vagus (X)

    • These do NOT match the names given in the question.

Conclusion:

The correct answer is:

Option B

$\boxed{\text{i - Vagus, ii - Auditory, iii - Trigeminal}}$

content:
Match the parts of human eye in Column I with their respective characteristics in Column II.

Column I
 Column II
i. Fovea Centralis a. Thick, Muscular ring like structure at junction of choroid and iris.
ii. Conjunctiva b. Area of retina from which the optic nerve and blood vessels leave the eyeball.
iii. Blind spot c. Exposed, transparent part of sclera and cornea.
iv. Ciliary body d. Place of formation of sharpest vision.

Choose the correct option from below.

options.content: i - d ii - c iii - b iv - a
i - d ii - c iii - a iv - b
i - b ii - d iii - c iv -a

explanation:
Let us match step by step:

  1. Fovea Centralis → It is the place on the retina where the sharpest vision is formed.
  • So, i → d
  1. Conjunctiva → It is the thin, transparent membrane that covers the exposed part of sclera and cornea.
  • So, ii → c
  1. Blind spot → It is the area on the retina from which the optic nerve and blood vessels leave the eyeball. No photoreceptor cells are present here.
  • So, iii → b
  1. Ciliary body → It is a thick, muscular, ring-like structure at the junction of choroid and iris.
  • So, iv → a

Final Matching:

  • i – d

  • ii – c

  • iii – b

  • iv – a

Correct Option: A ✅

content: With reference to role of internal ear in equilibrium receptors for dynamic balance of body lie in  i while the receptors for static or linear balance of body lie in  ii

options.content:
i ii
organ of corti tectorial membrane

i ii
cristae of ampulla maculae of utriculus and sacculus.

i ii
scala vestibule scala tympani

i ii
Reissner's membrane tectorial membrane

explanation:
To answer this question, let us recall the NCERT facts about equilibrium:

  1. Dynamic equilibrium (maintaining balance during movement or rotation) is detected by the cristae present in the ampullae of the semicircular canals.

  2. Static equilibrium (detecting head position and linear acceleration) is detected by maculae present in the utricle and saccule of the vestibule.

Checking each option:

  • Option A: Organ of Corti and tectorial membrane are for hearing, not equilibrium.

  • Option B: Cristae of ampulla (for dynamic balance), maculae of utriculus and sacculus (for static/linear balance).

  • Option C: Scala vestibule and scala tympani are parts of cochlea, related to hearing.

  • Option D: Reissner's membrane and tectorial membrane are within cochlea, related to hearing.

Correct Answer:

Option B is correct.

  • $i =$ cristae of ampulla

  • $ii =$ maculae of utriculus and sacculus

These are the equilibrium receptors in the internal ear as per the NCERT textbook.

content: Consumption of which drug increases the concentration of dopamine in the brain?
explanation:
The correct answer is:

✅ Option D — Cocaine

Explanation:

Cocaine increases the concentration of dopamine in the brain by blocking the reuptake (reabsorption) of dopamine into neurons. This leads to an accumulation of dopamine in the synaptic cleft, resulting in intense feelings of euphoria and stimulation.

Other options:

  • Heroin acts mainly on opioid receptors and increases dopamine indirectly, but not as directly as cocaine.

  • Hashish (cannabis) acts on cannabinoid receptors and has milder effects on dopamine.

  • Lysergic acid (LSD) primarily affects serotonin receptors rather than dopamine.

👉 Hence, Cocaine is the drug that most directly and significantly increases dopamine concentration in the brain.

options.content: Pia mater and choroid plexus only.
Pia mater, choroid plexus and ependymal cells lining the ventricles.
White matter, Pia mater and grey matter.

explanation:
Step 1: Recall where Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF) is formed.

  • CSF is mainly secreted by choroid plexuses present in the ventricles of the brain.

  • Choroid plexus is formed by invaginations of pia mater covered by ependymal cells.

  • Thus, the secretion of CSF involves the pia mater, choroid plexus, and ependymal cells lining ventricles.

Step 2: Match with options:

  • Option A: Only choroid plexus → Incomplete.

  • Option B: Pia mater + choroid plexus → Incomplete.

  • Option C: Pia mater, choroid plexus, and ependymal cells → Correct.

  • Option D: White matter, pia mater, grey matter → Incorrect.

Final Answer: Option C ✅

content:
Arrange the following in sequence during the transmission of nerve impulse at chemical synapse.

i. Neurotransmitter binds with receptors of post synaptic cell.

ii. Calcium channels open and the calcium diffuses inward from ECF.

iii. Impulse travels along the axon of presynaptic neuron.

iv. Release of neurotransmitter by exocytosis.

v. Synaptic vesicles fuse with cell membrane of axon terminal of presynaptic neuron.
explanation:
1. Impulse arrives at the axon terminal of the presynaptic neuron. (iii)

  2. Voltage-gated Ca²⁺ channels open and Ca²⁺ diffuses inward. (ii)

  3. Synaptic vesicles fuse with the presynaptic membrane. (v)

  4. Neurotransmitter is released into the synaptic cleft by exocytosis. (iv)

  5. Neurotransmitter binds to receptors on the postsynaptic cell. (i)

Sequence:

$$iii \;\to\; ii \;\to\; v \;\to\; iv \;\to\; i$$

Answer: Option D.

options.content: temporal lobe from frontal and parietal lobes
parietal lobe from occipital and frontal lobes

explanation:
1. Lateral sulcus (Sylvian fissure) is a deep groove on the lateral surface of the cerebral hemisphere.

  2. Above the fissure lie the frontal and parietal lobes.

  3. Below the fissure lies the temporal lobe.

Therefore, lateral sulcus separates the temporal lobe from the frontal and parietal lobes.

Correct answer: Option C.

content:
Match column I with column II and select the correct option.

Column I Column II
i. Frontal lobe a. Gustato receptors
ii. Parietal lobe b. Olfactory receptors
iii. Temporal lobe c. Sense of vision
iv. Occipital lobe d. Broca's area
explanation:
i. Frontal lobe → Broca’s area (speech production) → d

ii. Parietal lobe → Gustatory receptors (taste) → a

iii. Temporal lobe → Olfactory receptors (smell) → b

iv. Occipital lobe → Sense of vision → c

Correct option: D.
